content:
# Exodus Chapter 10 -- Disagreement Log

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Filed:** 2026-03-04

---

## 1. v.2 -- *Hit'alalti*: "wrought upon" vs. "dealt upon" vs. "made fools of"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "what I have wrought [made sport]" | Literal: dual rendering with bracketed alternative |
| LOGOS | "what I have dealt upon" | Formal: neutral verb |
| PNEUMA | "how I made fools of" | Dynamic: mockery emphasized |

**Hebrew:** הִתְעַלַּלְתִּי (*hit'alalti*) -- hitpael of *'alal* (עלל), "to deal with, to wrought upon, to make sport of." The root carries both severity and scorn.

**LXX:** ἐνέπαιξα (*enepaiza*) -- "I mocked, I made sport of." Supports the mockery dimension.

**Nature of disagreement:** The hitpael of *'alal* contains both severity (YHWH's actions are devastating) and mockery (YHWH has exposed Egyptian impotence). EMET captures both dimensions through dual rendering. LOGOS's "dealt upon" is too neutral -- it could describe any kind of action. PNEUMA's "made fools of" captures the mockery but loses the gravity: YHWH's actions were not pranks but catastrophic judgments. The verb also carries the sense of "wrought" -- making or fashioning something through powerful action.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "what I have wrought upon Egypt" -- taking EMET's primary rendering. "Wrought upon" is suitably archaic-elevated, conveying powerful action with overtones of both craftsmanship (YHWH fashioned these events) and severity. It leaves room for the mockery dimension without reducing YHWH's actions to it.

**Severity:** MODERATE. The verb choice shapes how readers understand YHWH's stance toward Egypt -- as devastating judge, as mocker of false gods, or both.

---

## 2. v.5 -- *Ein ha-arets*: "the eye of the land" vs. "the face of the ground"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the eye of the land" | Literal: preserves Hebrew metaphor |
| LOGOS | "the eye of the land" | Formal: preserves Hebrew metaphor |
| PNEUMA | "the face of the ground" | Dynamic: interprets *ein* as "face/surface" |

**Hebrew:** עֵין הָאָרֶץ (*ein ha-arets*) -- literally "the eye of the land." The word *ein* (עין) means "eye" as its primary sense; "surface, appearance" is a secondary/metaphorical extension.

**LXX:** τὴν ὄψιν τῆς γῆς (*tēn opsin tēs gēs*) -- "the appearance/face of the earth." Interprets *ein* toward "appearance."

**Nature of disagreement:** PNEUMA follows the LXX interpretive tradition, rendering *ein* as "face" -- losing the vivid Hebrew metaphor. The land has an "eye" that the locusts cover; this image anticipates the darkness plague where sight itself is abolished. EMET and LOGOS preserve the literal metaphor.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the eye of the land" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The metaphor is too striking and too thematically connected to the darkness plague to dissolve into the more generic "face."

**Severity:** MODERATE. The phrase appears twice in the locust narrative (vv. 5, 15) and its visual/optical resonance with the darkness plague is a key literary connection.

---

## 3. v.7 -- *Avdah Mitsrayim*: "is destroyed" vs. "is lost" vs. "is ruined"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Mitsrayim is lost" | Literal: *abad* can mean "to be lost, to perish" |
| LOGOS | "Egypt is destroyed" | Formal: emphatic finality |
| PNEUMA | "Egypt is ruined" | Dynamic: severe but reversible |

**Hebrew:** אָבְדָה מִצְרָיִם (*avdah Mitsrayim*) -- qal perfect of *abad* (אבד), "to perish, to be lost, to be destroyed." The perfect tense presents the destruction as completed fact.

**LXX:** ἀπόλωλεν Αἴγυπτος (*apolōlen Aiguptos*) -- "Egypt has perished." Perfect tense, matching the Hebrew.

**Nature of disagreement:** EMET's "lost" can sound merely misplaced -- a wallet is "lost," a country "perishes" or "is destroyed." LOGOS's "destroyed" captures the finality of the qal perfect. PNEUMA's "ruined" suggests severe damage but implies possible recovery -- the Hebrew *abad* is terminal.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Egypt is destroyed" -- following LOGOS. The verb *abad* in the qal perfect is unambiguously terminal. "Destroyed" conveys the finality the servants intend: Egypt is already finished.

**Severity:** LOW-MODERATE. The core meaning (Egypt is in catastrophic condition) is conveyed by all three, but the degree of finality matters for the servants' desperation.

---

## 4. v.8 -- *Mi vami*: "who and who" vs. "who exactly"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Who and who [are] the ones going?" | Literal: preserves doubled interrogative |
| LOGOS | "Who exactly are the ones going?" | Formal: interprets doubling as emphasis |
| PNEUMA | "But exactly who will be going?" | Dynamic: rephrased question |

**Hebrew:** מִי וָמִי הַהֹלְכִים (*mi vami haholkhim*) -- literally "who and who the going-ones?" The doubled interrogative is distinctive Hebrew idiom.

**LXX:** τίνες καὶ τίνες οἱ πορευόμενοι (*tines kai tines hoi poreuomenoi*) -- "who and who are the ones going?" Preserves the doubling.

**Nature of disagreement:** LOGOS and PNEUMA smooth the doubled interrogative into "exactly who" -- losing the Hebrew's distinctive rhetorical force. The doubling conveys Pharaoh's impatient, suspicious demand for specification. The LXX preserves it.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Who and who are the ones going?" -- following EMET. The doubled *mi vami* is too distinctive to normalize. English readers can parse "who and who" as an emphatic demand.

**Severity:** LOW. The meaning is identical across all three renderings. The disagreement concerns whether to preserve a Hebrew idiom.

---

## 5. v.10 -- Pharaoh's sarcasm: preserving vs. resolving ambiguity of *ra'ah*

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "See, for evil [is] before your faces" | Literal: ambiguous *ra'ah* preserved |
| LOGOS | "See, for evil is before your faces" | Formal: ambiguous *ra'ah* preserved |
| PNEUMA | "Clearly you have some evil scheme in mind" | Dynamic: resolves ambiguity toward "evil intent" |

**Hebrew:** רְאוּ כִּי רָעָה נֶגֶד פְּנֵיכֶם (*re'u ki ra'ah neged peneikhem*) -- "see, for evil [is] before/against your faces." The word *ra'ah* (רעה) means both "evil intent" and "calamity/disaster."

**LXX:** ἴδετε ὅτι πονηρία πρόσκειται ὑμῖν (*idete hoti ponēria prokeitai humin*) -- "see that evil intent lies before you." Resolves toward malicious intent.

**Nature of disagreement:** The Hebrew is deliberately ambiguous. Pharaoh may be accusing Moses of plotting evil, or warning that calamity awaits them, or both. PNEUMA follows the LXX's resolution toward "evil scheme." EMET and LOGOS preserve the ambiguity by rendering *ra'ah* as "evil" without specifying what kind.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "See, for evil is before your faces" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The ambiguity is the text's intention. Pharaoh's threat operates on multiple levels: accusation of evil intent, warning of calamity, and perhaps both simultaneously. The reader should encounter the same interpretive challenge.

**Severity:** MODERATE. The rendering determines whether the reader encounters Pharaoh as accuser (PNEUMA), as issuer of a multi-layered threat (EMET/LOGOS), or somewhere in between.

---

## 6. v.14 -- *Kaved me'od*: "very heavy" vs. "in devastating numbers" (locust swarm)

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "very heavy [kaved]" | Literal: preserves *kaved* with gloss |
| LOGOS | "very heavy" | Formal: preserves *kaved* |
| PNEUMA | "in devastating numbers" | Dynamic: interpretive, loses *kaved* |

**Hebrew:** כָּבֵד מְאֹד (*kaved me'od*) -- "heavy very." The adjective *kaved* is the same root used for heart-hardening throughout the narrative.

**Nature of disagreement:** The same structural issue as Exodus 9 (where PNEUMA rendered *kaved* plague descriptions as "devastating," "severe," etc.). The *kaved* saturation of Exodus 10 is extraordinary: YHWH makes the heart heavy (v. 1), the locust plague is heavy (v. 14), the rescue wind is "very strong" (v. 19, also *kaved me'od*). PNEUMA's "devastating numbers" severs this connection entirely.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "very heavy" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The *kaved* wordplay is architecturally essential to the chapter and the entire plague narrative.

**Severity:** MODERATE. Same reasoning as the *kaved* disagreements in Exodus 9. The literary architecture depends on the connection being visible.

---

## 7. v.21 -- *Veyamesh choshekh*: "darkness that may be felt" vs. "darkness to be felt" vs. "darkness so thick it can be touched"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "and one may feel [the] darkness" | Literal: active subject, brackets |
| LOGOS | "a darkness to be felt" | Formal: passive infinitive |
| PNEUMA | "a darkness so thick it can be touched" | Dynamic: adds "thick," narrows to "touched" |

**Hebrew:** וְיָמֵשׁ חֹשֶׁךְ (*veyamesh choshekh*) -- from *mashash* (משׁש), "to feel, to grope." The verb means the darkness is so dense it has physical presence -- it can be felt, groped, encountered tactilely.

**LXX:** ψηλαφητὸν σκότος (*psēlaphēton skotos*) -- "palpable/gropeable darkness." Confirms the tactile reading.

**Nature of disagreement:** This is one of the most debated phrases in Exodus. The three renderings differ in structure rather than meaning: all convey that the darkness is tactile. EMET's "one may feel [the] darkness" is awkward with the bracketed article. LOGOS's "a darkness to be felt" is elegant but somewhat passive. PNEUMA's "so thick it can be touched" adds two elements not in the Hebrew: (1) "thick" (reserved for *afelah* in v. 22), and (2) "touched" (narrower than *mashash*, which encompasses groping and general tactile perception, not just touching).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "a darkness that may be felt" -- a formulation that draws on English translation tradition while preserving the Hebrew's full tactile range. "May be felt" keeps the modal force of the Hebrew (*veyamesh* as a jussive or imperfect) and does not prematurely introduce "thick" (which belongs to v. 22's *afelah*).

**Severity:** HIGH. This is one of the defining images of the plague narrative. The rendering must convey the supernatural, tangible quality of the darkness without over-interpreting or under-interpreting the Hebrew verb.

---

## 8. v.22 -- *Choshekh-afelah*: "thick darkness" vs. "smothering darkness"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "afelah-choshekh [thick darkness]" | Literal: transliterated, reversed order |
| LOGOS | "thick darkness" | Formal: standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "a smothering darkness" | Dynamic: metaphorical interpretation |

**Hebrew:** חֹשֶׁךְ־אֲפֵלָה (*choshekh-afelah*) -- a compound of two darkness words. *Choshekh* is the common term; *afelah* (from *'apal*, "to be dark, thick, gloomy") is the intensifier -- dense, impenetrable murk.

**LXX:** σκότος γνόφος θύελλα (*skotos gnophos thuella*) -- "darkness, murk, storm" -- expanding *afelah* into two terms.

**Nature of disagreement:** EMET reverses the Hebrew order (the Hebrew reads *choshekh-afelah*, EMET renders "afelah-choshekh") and adds a transliteration that is unnecessary. PNEUMA's "smothering" imports a metaphor (darkness as suffocating agent) that is evocative but not in the Hebrew. The text says the darkness is *thick*, not that it *smothers*.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "a thick darkness" -- following LOGOS. "Thick" is the standard English rendering of *afelah* and captures the intensification without adding metaphorical content not present in the Hebrew.

**Severity:** MODERATE. The distinction between "thick" and "smothering" is a difference between describing a quality (LOGOS) and attributing agency to the darkness (PNEUMA).

---

## 9. v.23 -- "Each man his brother" vs. "one another" -- Hebrew idiom preservation

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "They did not see, each man his brother" | Literal: preserves *ish et-akhiv* |
| LOGOS | "They did not see one another" | Formal: natural English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "No one could see another person" | Dynamic: fully paraphrased |

**Hebrew:** לֹא־רָאוּ אִישׁ אֶת־אָחִיו (*lo ra'u ish et-akhiv*) -- "they did not see, each man his brother." The idiom *ish et-akhiv* ("a man his brother") is the standard Hebrew way of expressing reciprocity.

**Nature of disagreement:** LOGOS and PNEUMA naturalize the Hebrew idiom into English equivalents. EMET preserves the Hebrew structure. The question is whether the idiom "each man his brother" carries meaning beyond the reciprocal function -- specifically, whether the *akh* ("brother") language creates a thematic connection to the fracturing of Egyptian society (they cannot even see their "brothers," i.e., fellow Egyptians).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Mixed: "They did not see, each man his brother" for the first clause (preserving the idiom per EMET), then "and they did not rise, each man from his place" (preserving EMET's structure for the parallel clause). The Hebrew parallelism (*ish et-akhiv* // *ish mittakhtav*) is preserved.

**Severity:** LOW. The reciprocal meaning is identical. The disagreement concerns whether to preserve a Hebrew idiom that has modest thematic resonance.

---

## 10. v.25 -- "Give into our hands" vs. "provide us with" -- *titten beyadenu*

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Also you shall give into our hand sacrifices and burnt offerings" | Literal: Hebrew hand imagery |
| LOGOS | "You yourself must also give into our hands sacrifices and burnt offerings" | Formal: emphatic *gam-attah*, hand imagery |
| PNEUMA | "You yourself must also provide us with sacrifices and burnt offerings" | Dynamic: "provide" for "give into hand" |

**Hebrew:** גַּם־אַתָּה תִּתֵּן בְּיָדֵנוּ זְבָחִים וְעֹלוֹת (*gam-attah titten beyadenu zevakhim ve'olot*) -- "also you, you shall give into our hand sacrifices and burnt offerings."

**Nature of disagreement:** PNEUMA's "provide" is a reasonable English equivalent but loses the concrete Hebrew imagery: Pharaoh must *give into Moses' hand*. The act of giving into the hand is theologically significant -- it implies direct transfer of possession, not merely supplying. The hand imagery also connects to the "hand of YHWH" motif running through the plague narrative.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "You yourself must also give into our hands sacrifices and burnt offerings" -- following LOGOS. The *beyadenu* ("into our hands") imagery is preserved.

**Severity:** LOW. The meaning is equivalent. The disagreement concerns whether to preserve a concrete Hebrew idiom.

---

## 11. v.29 -- *Ken dibbarta*: "rightly" vs. "well" -- rendering of *ken*

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "You have spoken rightly" | Literal: *ken* = "rightly, correctly" |
| LOGOS | "You have spoken rightly" | Formal: same |
| PNEUMA | "You have spoken well" | Dynamic: "well" for *ken* |

**Hebrew:** כֵּן דִּבַּרְתָּ (*ken dibbarta*) -- "rightly/correctly you have spoken." The adverb *ken* means "so, thus, rightly, correctly" -- it affirms the correctness of what was said.

**LXX:** εἴρηκας (*eirēkas*) -- "you have spoken." Omits the affirmative adverb entirely.

**Nature of disagreement:** PNEUMA renders *ken* as "well," which in English connotes quality or eloquence ("you spoke well") rather than correctness ("you spoke rightly"). The Hebrew *ken* means "correctly, accurately" -- Moses is affirming the truth of Pharaoh's statement, not praising its quality. The dramatic irony depends on this: Moses says "you have spoken *correctly*" because Pharaoh's words are truer than he knows.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "You have spoken rightly" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The adverb *ken* means "correctly," not "well."

**Severity:** LOW. The overall sense (Moses affirms Pharaoh's words with irony) is clear in all three renderings. But "rightly" sharpens the dramatic irony.

---

## 12. Throughout -- PNEUMA's *kaved* wordplay dissolution (chapter-wide pattern)

As in Exodus 9, PNEUMA renders *kaved* differently across the chapter, breaking the literary connection between plague severity and heart-stubbornness:

| Verse | Hebrew | EMET/LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10:1 | *hikhbadti et-libbo* | "I have made heavy his heart" | "I have made his heart heavy" |
| 10:14 | *kaved me'od* | "very heavy" | "devastating numbers" |

The first instance (v. 1) is a minor word-order difference and not problematic. The v. 14 instance, however, entirely severs the *kaved* connection between the plague description and the heart-hardening verb in v. 1. In a chapter where YHWH "makes heavy" (*hikhbadti*) Pharaoh's heart (v. 1) and the resulting locust plague is "very heavy" (*kaved me'od*, v. 14), the wordplay is architecturally central -- the heaviness of the judgment answers the heaviness of the resistance. PNEUMA's "devastating numbers" eliminates this.

**Severity:** MODERATE (cumulative). The pattern continues from Exodus 9 and will likely continue through Exodus 11-12.

---

## 13. Recurring convention disagreements

The following disagreements recur from previous chapters and are noted briefly:

**a. Divine name:** LOGOS uses "the LORD"; EMET/PNEUMA use YHWH. SOPHIA follows YHWH per established convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**b. Pronoun capitalization for God:** LOGOS capitalizes divine pronouns (My, Mine, He, Me); EMET and PNEUMA do not. SOPHIA follows lowercase convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**c. "Sons of Israel" vs. "Israelites":** EMET: "sons of Yisra'el"; LOGOS: "sons of Israel"; PNEUMA: "Israelites" / "the Israelites." SOPHIA follows "sons of Israel" per convention where the Hebrew reads *benei Yisra'el*. (Recurring, LOW)

**d. Hebrew names:** EMET uses transliterated forms (Mosheh, Par'oh, Aharon, Mitsrayim, Yisra'el); LOGOS and PNEUMA use standard English forms (Moses, Pharaoh, Aaron, Egypt, Israel). SOPHIA follows standard English forms per convention. (Recurring, LOW)

---

*Thirteen disagreements logged for Exodus 10 (plus recurring conventions). One rated HIGH (veyamesh choshekh rendering in v. 21), one rated MODERATE-cumulative (PNEUMA's kaved wordplay dissolution), five rated MODERATE (hit'alalti in v. 2; ein ha-arets in v. 5; ra'ah ambiguity in v. 10; kaved me'od in v. 14; choshekh-afelah in v. 22), and five rated LOW or LOW-MODERATE (avdah in v. 7; mi vami in v. 8; ish et-akhiv in v. 23; titten beyadenu in v. 25; ken dibbarta in v. 29; recurring conventions). The dominant pattern from chapters 8-9 continues: EMET and LOGOS converge on literal/formal renderings, PNEUMA diverges toward interpretive paraphrase. Notable in this chapter: greater consensus among all three translators on the hardening verbs (all render kaved hiphil as "made heavy" and chazaq piel as "strengthened"), likely because the conventions are now well-established. The primary disagreements center on literary preservation (eye of the land, kaved wordplay, Hebrew idioms) and the rendering of the darkness imagery (vv. 21-22).*

--- SOPHIA
